did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep transfer learning models, specifically a two-path architecture combining EfficientNet and MobileNetV2 as base feature extractors, along with ensemble learning techniques, to perform 8-class classification of skin diseases (including skin cancers) using the ISIC-2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' and 'Research Paper'. It presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a two-path deep transfer learning model (combining EfficientNet and MobileNetV2) for 8-class skin disease classification using the ISIC-2019 dataset. The paper includes specific methodology, results (e.g., 70% accuracy), and discussion of new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin disease classification using two path deep transfer learning models
journal: Journal of Knowledge Learning and Science Technology
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of diagnosing skin diseases by developing a computationally efficient two-path deep transfer learning model for 8-class classification, including skin cancers. Utilizing the ISIC-2019 dataset, the authors combined EfficientNetB2V2 and MobileNetV2 as base feature extractors, processing differently augmented images through each path to handle class imbalance and enhance feature extraction. The model was trained and evaluated using standard metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. The proposed ensemble approach achieved a test accuracy of 70%, with a recall of 0.75 and an F1 score of approximately 0.667. The results indicate that this dual-path architecture offers a reliable, low-computational alternative to state-of-the-art models that typically require higher processing power.

all_task_types.target: 8-class skin disease diagnosis (Melanoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Vascular Lesion, Actinic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin diseases using the ISIC-2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (one of eight classes) using a two-path deep transfer learning architecture (EfficientNetB2V2 and MobileNetV2) combined with ensemble learning. The task is explicitly defined as '8-class identification of skin diseases' and 'multi class classification'. Metrics reported include accuracy (70%), recall (0.75), precision (0.625), and F1 score (0.667).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: The primary dataset used for the study, described as the largest collection of dermatoscopic images available at the time, serving as a benchmark and industry standard for evaluating models. It contains images classified into eight different classes.

use.training: Used for training the two-path deep transfer learning model (EfficientNetB2V2 and MobileNetV2) after segmentation into classes and application of oversampling/undersampling and image augmentation techniques to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance, achieving a final accuracy of 70%.
use.other: The paper notes that the ISIC 2020 dataset was not used because it consisted of only 7 classes and focused primarily on benign and melanoma lesions, which would have limited the scope of the 8-class research.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A dataset mentioned in the context of discussing class imbalance issues and comparing with other studies. It contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images collected from the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna, Austria, and the skin cancer practice of Cliff Rosendahl in Queensland, Australia.

use.other: Used as a reference example to illustrate the problem of class imbalance in dermatological datasets (e.g., comparing NV and DF counts) and cited in comparison with other research papers (e.g., Rahman et al., 2021).

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset mentioned in the context of class imbalance and comparison with other studies. It is described as a large dataset maintained by a diverse group of dermatologists with images from various patients and locations.

use.other: Used as a reference example to illustrate class imbalance (NV vs DF counts) and cited in comparison with other research papers (e.g., Esteva et al., 2017).

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset made in collaboration with the University of Porto, University of London, and Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano. It is a detailed high-quality annotated dataset containing manual segmentation of skin lesions, clinical diagnosis, and identification of features.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using standard metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) and compared qualitatively with other studies.

ISIC-2019 Challenge Dataset.notes: The model was trained for 10 epochs. The confusion matrix indicated confusion between AK (Actinic Keratosis) and DF (Dermatofibroma) classes due to lack of distinct features. VASC class performance improved to 93% due to augmentation. The paper notes that multi-class classification from ISIC dataset gives significantly lower results compared to binary tasks due to similar features like inflammation and texture across classes.

Rahman et al., 2021 [72].method: Ensemble learning with weighted averaging (ResNeXt, SeResNeXt, ResNet, Xception, DenseNet)

Catarina Barata et al., 2019 [74].method: Hierarchical DenseNet-161 and Attention ResNet-Inception (CNN-LSTM) and Ensembling

Nils Gessert et al., 2018 [75].method: Transfer Learning, Ensembling, Meta-Learning (54 models including SENet154, ResNeXt101, Densenet variants, SE-Resnet101, PolyNet)

Amirreza Mahbod et al., 2020 [76].method: Transfer Learning, Ensembling (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, SeResNeXt-50) Multi-Scale CNN 3 level fusion

Sara Atito Ali Ahmed et al., 2020 [77].method: Transfer Learning, Model Fine-tuning, Ensembling (Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, NasNetLarge, UNK)

Josef Steppan et al., 2021 [78].method: Transfer Learning, Model Fine Tuning, Ensembling (EfficientNet-B5, SE-ResNeXt-101, EfficientNet-B4, Inception-ResNet-v2)

Comparison with Other Studies (Table 3).notes: The proposed model's accuracy of 70.3% (listed in Table 3 as 70.3% but 70% in text) is compared against these prior works. The paper states the proposed model outperforms many models in terms of training accuracy on ISIC-2019 while maintaining lower computational complexity.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-path deep transfer learning ensemble model combining EfficientNetB2V2 and MobileNetV2
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (standard for EfficientNetV2S and MobileNetV2)

architecture_details.path_structure: Two-path architecture where two distinctly preprocessed images from the same source are fed into separate base models (EfficientNetB2V2 and MobileNetV2) to extract features.

feature_extraction_modifications.EfficientNetB2V2: Last fully connected classification layers removed. Added a convolution layer with 3x3 kernel and 128 filters, followed by a 2x2 max pooling layer. Output flattened.
feature_extraction_modifications.MobileNetV2: Last 30 layers unfrozen for fine-tuning; other layers kept frozen initially.
feature_extraction_modifications.MobileNetV2_architecture_note: Uses pointwise and depth-wise convolutions with ReLU activation and batch normalization in an inverted bottleneck block.

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Stacking/Ensemble learning combining outputs from both base models.
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected dense layers with a final softmax activation layer for 8-class probability output.

training_details.epochs: 10 epochs (initial training with frozen layers) + 10 epochs (fine-tuning with unfrozen layers)
training_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Initial phase: Base model layers frozen. Fine-tuning phase: Last 30 layers of MobileNetV2 unfrozen; EfficientNetB2V2 layers also unfrozen for fine-tuning with low learning rate.
training_details.regularization: Early stopping callback used to save weights based on best validation accuracy.
training_details.data_augmentation: Simple image augmentation applied to handle class imbalance; two distinctly processed images created from each initial image.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected dense layers with Softmax activation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Two-path deep transfer learning model (EfficientNetB2V2 + MobileNetV2 ensemble)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of pixel values to range 0-1
Noise and hair removal using a median filter with kernel size 9
Histogram Equalization (specifically Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization - CLAHE with clipLimit of 3.8 and tile size of 6x6) to improve contrast
Image segmentation to create masks separating the foreground (skin lesion) from the background (surrounding tissue)
Filtration (general filtering techniques applied to create distinctly processed images for the two-path model)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Oversampling was applied to smaller classes (AK, DF, VASC, SCC) and undersampling was applied to larger classes (NV) to match sample quantities and prevent bias toward larger classes.

Applied to handle class imbalance. The paper notes that 'simple image augmentation' was run first. Specific techniques mentioned in the context of previous research and potential implementation include: Translation (shifting along x and y axis randomly), Scaling (resizing/cropping by randomized value), Rotation (randomized angle), Flipping (horizontal, vertical, or both), and Noise Addition (Gaussian noise).

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Distinct Image Processing for Two-Path Input
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Two distinctly processed images were created from each initial image to feed into the two-path model. This involved applying dissimilar preprocessing techniques (such as different augmentations or transformations) to the same source image to extract different features.
Listed as a color image processing technique used for augmentation, specifically mentioned in the context of manipulating RGB/HSRB formats.
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization was used as a preprocessing/augmentation step to improve contrast. Parameters: clipLimit of 3.8 and tile size of 6x6.

conclusions: The proposed two-path deep transfer learning model, combining EfficientNetB2V2 and MobileNetV2 as base feature extractors with ensemble learning, achieved a test accuracy of 70% on the ISIC-2019 dataset for 8-class skin disease classification.
The model demonstrated a recall of 0.75, a precision of 0.625, and an F1 score of approximately 0.667.

The two-path architecture, which processes two distinctly preprocessed images from the same source through separate base models, allows the classifier to consider different features that become prominent due to dissimilar preprocessing techniques.
The proposed model offers a computationally cheaper alternative to state-of-the-art models that typically require higher computational power, while maintaining accuracy close to those complex models.
The model achieved a training accuracy of 95.23% and a validation accuracy of 68% after 10 epochs.
Class imbalance in the dataset led to favored predictions for classes with more images; this was addressed via simple image augmentation and creating two distinctly processed images per source image.
The Vascular Lesion (VASC) class performance improved to 93% accuracy due to augmentation, as it had limited initial data.
The model showed confusion between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Dermatofibroma (DF) classes, attributed to a lack of distinctly recognizable features and high intra-class variation in the data.

Multi-class classification from the ISIC dataset yields significantly lower results compared to binary tasks due to overlapping features like inflammation and texture across different disease classes.

The authors conclude that further improvements in data gathering and preprocessing techniques, along with exploring other base transfer learning models, could make the results more reliable while maintaining low computational requirements.

The study highlights that two-path algorithms employing simpler models are useful for multi-class classification tasks where differently processed images are required to extract features of distinct diseases.
important_remarks: The model achieved a test accuracy of only 70% on the ISIC-2019 dataset, which is lower than several state-of-the-art models cited in the comparison table (e.g., 88.0% by Rahman et al., 92.9% by Polat et al., and 97.0% by Gessert et al.).
The study relies exclusively on the ISIC-2019 dataset without external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting the generalizability of the findings.

The paper acknowledges significant class imbalance in the ISIC-2019 dataset (e.g., 12,000 images for Melanocytic Nevus vs. 239 for Dermatofibroma), which inherently biases predictions toward majority classes despite augmentation efforts.

The model exhibits confusion between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Dermatofibroma (DF) classes due to overlapping visual features (inflammation, texture) and lack of distinct characteristics in the data.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported to validate the significance of the performance differences compared to other models.

The dataset used for testing was created by the authors ('test data was created containing images from different classes') rather than using a held-out portion of the original ISIC-2019 challenge split, raising potential concerns about data leakage or non-standard evaluation protocols.

The study does not report per-class performance metrics (precision, recall, F1) for all eight classes, only providing aggregate accuracy and specific metrics for the VASC class (93% accuracy), making it difficult to assess performance on rare or critical classes like Melanoma.

The computational efficiency claims are qualitative; no specific metrics such as FLOPs, inference time, or model size are provided to substantiate the claim of 'lower computational complexity' compared to the cited SOTA models.

The authors admit that multi-class classification from the ISIC dataset yields significantly lower results than binary tasks due to feature overlap, suggesting the model's utility for broad screening may be limited.
The paper does not address potential demographic biases in the ISIC-2019 dataset regarding skin tone, age, or geographic origin, which is a critical factor for dermatological AI deployment.
